coredata.dc:title: Green production of fluorescent carbon quantum dots based on pine wood and its application in the detection of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Siyu
Song, Xueping
Chai, Xinyu
Zhao, Peitao
He, Hui
Liu, Zhenwei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have novel applications as fluorescent nanomaterials in various fields. In this study, a green hydrothermal carbonisation (HTC) method is proposed to produce CQDs from pine wood (P-CQDs). The P-CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence characteristics, with the optimal excitation wavelength being 330 nm and a corresponding emission wavelength of 447 nm. A blue light emission under ultraviolet light was observed and a typical graphene structure was seen in the HR-TEM images with a fringe spacing of 0.21 nm. Moreover, the mechanism of P-CQD formation during the HTC process was proposed. It was observed that P-CQDs demonstrated an aromatic polymer structure rich in oxygen-containing groups. This is because of the condensation, chain breaking, and chain forming reactions occurring among the hydrolytic products of the cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin in pine wood. The fluorescence quenching results indicated that the P-CQDs selectively detected Fe3+ in an aqueous phase. This implies that these CQDs have broad application prospects for Fe3+ detection. More importantly, this signifies that the large-scale and low-cost production of CQDs and Fe3+ sensors will now be possible.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Pine wood
Fluorescence characteristic
Formation mechanism
Fe3+ sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of pine wood carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs)
Performance of P-CQDs during Fe3+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images (the inner image was detected under high resolution), (b) particle size distribution, and AFM images (c, d) of P-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of P-CQDs (inset: photographs of the P-CQDs under sunlight and UV light), (b) FL emission spectra of P-CQDs, and (c) the fluorescence intensity of P-CQDs under different pH values.
(a) XPS wide scan profile, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s of high-resolution XPS spectra, and (e) FT-IR spectrum of P-CQDs.

(a) Changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of P-CQDs after the addition of various metal ions, (b) Fluorescence spectra of P-CQDs in different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–2000 μmol/L), (c) Linear relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration, and (d) P-CQDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ under 365 nm UV light.

head.ce:title: Green production of fluorescent carbon quantum dots based on pine wood and its application in the detection of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical and Power Engineering
China University of Mining and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical and Power Engineering, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have novel applications as fluorescent nanomaterials in various fields. In this study, a green hydrothermal carbonisation (HTC) method is proposed to produce CQDs from pine wood (P-CQDs). The P-CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence characteristics, with the optimal excitation wavelength being 330 nm and a corresponding emission wavelength of 447 nm. A blue light emission under ultraviolet light was observed and a typical graphene structure was seen in the HR-TEM images with a fringe spacing of 0.21 nm. Moreover, the mechanism of P-CQD formation during the HTC process was proposed. It was observed that P-CQDs demonstrated an aromatic polymer structure rich in oxygen-containing groups. This is because of the condensation, chain breaking, and chain forming reactions occurring among the hydrolytic products of the cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin in pine wood. The fluorescence quenching results indicated that the P-CQDs selectively detected Fe3+ in an aqueous phase. This implies that these CQDs have broad application prospects for Fe3+ detection. More importantly, this signifies that the large-scale and low-cost production of CQDs and Fe3+ sensors will now be possible.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignocellulose materials such as bamboo, bagasse, wheat straw, and wood are important components of biomass resources, whose high value of utilisation has attracted significant attention in recent years. Hydrothermal carbonisation (HTC) process, as a common biomass refining method, has certain advantages such as simple operation, low cost, environmental compliance, and non-toxicity (Gu et al., 2019). However, in previous studies, the mechanism of the formation and the structure of the products obtained from HTC have not been properly analyzed. Wu et al. (2017) analyzed the HTC of loblolly pine and proposed its reaction mechanism and product structure; however, they did not consider the exact structure of the products, which inspired us to develop the present study. The hydrothermal liquid products of the lignocellulose material obtained from the HTC process include glycan, monosaccharide, lignin monomer, and their condensation products, as well as carbon quantum dots (CQDs). However, few studies on the mechanism of formation and overall structural analysis of CQDs so that they can be applied as novel fluorescent materials have been conducted. Thus, the work in this study will provide ideas for the preparation of CQDs with more carbon precursors and ideas for the structural analysis on the photoluminescence mechanism of CQDs.

When CQDs were first discovered in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), they attracted significant attention globally. CQDs have numerous unique advantages, such as good biocompatibility (Gu et al., 2016), low toxicity (Li et al., 2013), stable chemical properties (Chai et al., 2019), and good water solubility (Gao et al., 2019) as compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots (Reshma and Mohanan, 2019). Thus far, broad applications of CQDs have been proposed in various areas, including biological imaging (Zhou et al., 2013), fluorescence probes (He et al., 2016; Qiao et al., 2018), drug delivery (Feng et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2013), and heavy metal ion detection (Huang et al., 2018). The properties of the CQDs prepared from different biomass materials are different, which determines the diversity of their fields of application. Thus, a significant variety of raw materials to be used in the production of CQDs have also been actively explored. Lv et al. (2017) produced a nitrogen-doped GQD with citric acid and ethylene thane as the carbon precursors via hydrothermal treatment, where the CQDs were added to a culture of bacterial cellulose to result in a self-assembled reaction on the surface. The product had good fluorescence characteristics. Murugan et al. (2019) prepared CQDs that exhibited selectivity to Cu2+ with Eleusine coracana via a chemical oxidation treatment. Amit Kumar et al. (2017) prepared CQDs with Ocimum sanctum as a raw material via a hydrothermal treatment, which were selective to Pb2+ and applied in the live cellular imaging of MDA-MB 468. In these reports, the raw materials used were mostly purified substances or expensive food and drugs, leading to a high cost of production of the CQDs. In contrast, lignocellulose materials, which widely exist in nature, have greater potential in the large-scale production of CQDs. Their advantages include not only a high yield and easy attainment, but also low cost. China produced 88.11 million m3 of timber in 2018. Pine wood is not only one of the most common softwood species in northern China, it has also formed a material production industry chain in the field of paper making and other industries, making it easier to achieve the large-scale production of CQDs. Therefore, mechanism of the formation and the production of CQDs with pine wood is worth further exploration.

When Fe3+, a common heavy metal ion, accumulates in water at over 0.3 mg/L (Liu et al., 2015) it is harmful to the human body and natural water environments. If the amount of Fe3+ in the human body is too high, people will suffer from Methemoglobinemia (Funke et al., 2018), reducing the oxygen transport capacity of the blood and harming human health. At the same time, when the amount of Fe3+ in natural water is too high, the color of the water will significantly deepen, accompanied by a distinct metallic smell, causing water pollution. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ is important. Traditional methods for detecting heavy metal ions include ultraviolet spectroscopy (Anbinder et al., 2019), complexometric titration (Venezia et al., 2008), and atomic absorption spectrometry (Borahan et al., 2019; Muhammad et al., 2019). Most of these methods have certain disadvantages, such as large errors, a difficult fabrication, and a lengthy timeframe. Because some heavy metal ions can cause a fluorescence quenching effect of CQDs, a fluorescence quenching method can be used as a new method for detecting heavy metal ions. There have been numerous reports on the detection of heavy metal ions via CQDs fluorescence quenching. Atchudan et al. (2018) used phyllanthus acidus as a raw material to produce nitrogen-doped CQDs, which can be used as a fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of Fe3+. In addition, Yang et al. (2017) exposed mangosteen pulp to chemical oxidation, and produced CQDs that exhibit high selectivity to Fe3+. Huang et al. (2018) used a microwave method to treat dried bauhinia flowers, the subsequent product can be used as a fluorescence sensor to detect Fe3+ in biological samples. Although the CQDs used in these experiments are selective to Fe3+ ions, the raw materials used by the authors are generally expensive. To realize the mass production of an Fe3+ detector based on fluorescence quenching, it is a better choice to prepare CQDs from a pine lignocellulose material with a lower price.

In this study, we propose a simple, environmentally friendly, economical, and one-pot method to prepare CQDs from pine wood. As a precursor, the production of pine has formed a complete industrial chain, which provides the economic basis and possibility for large-scale and low-cost production of CQDs. Moreover, the formation mechanism of the pine wood carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) has been focused on, and it can be available to explain the formation of lignocellulose-based CQDs to some extent. The results will provide a theoretical basis for improving the yield of CQDs and explaining the photoluminescence properties of CQDs. At the same time, the P-CQDs were applied in the detection of 12 types of metal salts in water by judging their fluorescence quenching characteristics. These types of P-CQDs prepared from a cheap biomass have a broad application in the field of large-scale Fe3+ detector preparation.

Pine wood was obtained from a paper mill in Guangxi Province, China. Before the experiments, the wood was crushed into 200 meshes using a pulverizer and then dried for 12 h at 105 °C. Twelve metal salts, namely, CdCl2, FeSO4·7H2O, FeCl3, AgNO3, SnCl2·2H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, HgCl2, MnSO4·H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, Pb(NO3)2, MgSO4·7H2O, and KCl, were bought from Aladdin Reagent, Co., Ltd. (Shanghai). A phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution of pH = 7 was prepared using NaH2PO4·2H2O and Na2HPO4.7H2O, which were purchased from Aladdin Reagent, Co., Ltd. (Shanghai). The ultrapure water used in the experiments was obtained from Merck Milli-Q ultrapure water purification system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pine wood carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The P-CQDs were prepared from pine wood using a typical hydrothermal treatment. First, 3 g of pine wood was mixed with 15 mL of ultrapure water and placed in a 50 mL Teflon reactor. During the reaction, the sample was heated from room temperature to 180 °C at a rate of 3 °C/min and maintained for 3 h. The reactor was then immediately cooled to room temperature using ice water. The product obtained was washed using ultra-pure water, and the supernatant was then separated from the solid residue using a high-speed centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant obtained was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 500 Da) for 3 days, and the P-CQDs were obtained and stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

To study the selectivity of P-CQDs to metal ions, appropriate concentrations of different metal ions (K+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+) were diluted in a 5 mL PBS solution in order to reduce the pH fluctuation caused by the hydrolysis of the metal ions. A 5 mL solution of P-CQDs was then added to the metal ion solution (the concentration of metal ions in the mixed solution was 1000 μmol/L), and the reaction was allowed to take place at room temperature for 15 min. In addition, the chosen metal ion was used in a further experiment to test the effects of its concentration (0–2000 μmol/L) on the P-CQDs by examining the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and ion concentration.

The quantum yield (QY) of P-CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate (Dissolved in 0.05 mol/L sulfuric acid, with a quantum yield of 0.54) as a reference material. At the excitation wavelength of 330 nm, the quantum yield of P-CQDs was calculated as following formula:
Q
Y
P - C Q D s
= Q
Y
q s
·
S
P − C Q D s
S
q s
·
A
q s
A
P − C Q D s
·
η 2
P − C Q D s
η 2
q s

In the formula, “QY” represents the quantum yield, “qs” represents the quinine sulfate reference solution, “S” represents the integral area of the fluorescence peak in the PL spectrum, “A” represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and “η” represents the refractive index of solution.

The functional groups of P-CQDs were tested using a Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) (Brucker TENSOR II). The composition of the P-CQDs was analyzed using an elemental analyzer (PerkinElmer 2400II, American). The morphology of the P-CQDs was observed using a transmission electron microscope (FEI TECNAI G2 F30, American). In addition, the 3D structure of the P-CQDs was tested using an atomic force microscope (Hitachi 5100N, Japan). The fluorescence characteristics of the P-CQDs were then tested using a fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer (Hitachi RF-5301PC, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra of the P-CQDs were tested using a spectrophotometer (Hitachi u-4100 UV–vis, Japan). The carbon and oxygen functional groups and their contents on the surfaces of the P-CQDs were analyzed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS).

The micromorphology and particle size distribution of the P-CQDs were evaluated using a TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 (a) , the P-CQDs were circular and evenly dispersed at a lower resolution, and had a lattice structure with a 0.21 nm fringe spacing observed at a higher resolution, which corresponds to that of the graphene (100) planes (Wang et al., 2019). In addition, the 3.56 nm average diameter of the P-CQDs was calculated by measuring the diameters of 100 particles, which equaled. The results are shown in Fig. 1 (b). To further examine the structure of the P-CQDs, their 3D morphology was evaluated using an AFM. As shown in Fig. 1(c and d), the average height of the P-CQDs was 2.8 nm, corresponding to 5–7 layers of graphene. They also had a clear circular shape in a 2D AFM image, which is consistent with the TEM analysis results.

The optical properties were studied from the UV–vis and FL spectra. The fluorescence quantum yield of P-CQDs was calculated, and its QY was 4.69%. According to the UV–vis spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) , the P-CQDs had a wide absorption between 200 and 700 nm, and a weak absorption peak at 280 nm, indicating a π-π∗ transition, which may be related to the CC bond. A weak absorption peak at 330 nm suggested the existence of a n-π∗ transition, which was attributed to the CO structure in the P-CQDs. In addition, the inset in Fig. 2 (a) shows the images of the P-CQDs solutions taken under visible and ultraviolet light of 365 nm wavelength, which demonstrate a faint yellow color under visible light and emit a distinct blue light under UV light. Further optical characteristics of the P-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 (b). Combined with the results in Fig. 2 (a), the optimal excitation wavelength of the P-CQDs was 330 nm, and the emission wavelength was 447 nm, which was located in the blue region of the visible spectrum. Therefore, a wavelength of 330 nm was chosen for further experiments. A distinct red-shift in the emission wavelength of the P-CQDs was observed with an excitation wavelength of 310–400 nm. This phenomenon might suggest the presence of polar groups (such as –OH and –COOH) on the aromatic polymer structure in the CQDs. This makes the electron transition incur a relaxation phenomenon in polar solvents, such as ultra-pure water, thus leading to a difference in the fluorescence emission time at different excitation wavelengths under the time scale (Cushing et al., 2013). In addition, the fluorescence stability of the P-CQDs could be maintained well in 60 days, which showed that the fluorescence intensity of the P-CQDs was stable and could be maintained for a long time.

To determine the best environment for further application, the fluorescence stability of P-CQDs at different pH values was studied. As shown in Fig. 3 (c) , when the pH increased from 2 to 4, the fluorescence intensity of the P-CQDs at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm was slightly increased. In addition, when the pH increased from 4 to 12, the fluorescence intensity of the P-CQDs remained stable. The results indicate that the P-CQDs were stable under weak acidic, neutral, and alkaline environments, and that their intensity is not affected within a large pH range.

The chemical compositions and surface functional groups of P-CQDs were determined using an elemental analyzer (EA), FT-IR, and XPS. The results of the EA showed that the P-CQDs were rich in C (40.23 wt%) and O (41.65 wt%). In addition, small amounts of H (6.12 wt%) and N (2.03 wt%) were also detected. Similar results were also confirmed through a wide-scan XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), there are three main peaks, which are related to C, N, and O, respectively, suggesting that the P-CQDs mainly consisted of these three elements. Furthermore, peak deconvolutions of the three elements were conducted to further interpret their nature. In Fig. 3 (b), the high-resolution spectrum of C1s shows three main peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.13 eV, which are related to CC (59.90%), C–O (28.04%), and O–CO (12.06%), respectively. The results indicate that the P-CQDs are mainly composed of CC and are rich in C–O and O–CO, which implies that the P-CQDs might have oxygen-rich functional groups based on the structure of the CC. As shown in Fig. 3 (c), the N1s demonstrates two main peaks at 399.53 and 401.51 eV, respectively, which means that nitrogen atoms exist in the form of C–N–C and N–H. The results in Fig. 3(a and b) indicate that the P-CQDs exhibit a pyrrole nitrogen structure. In addition, the spectrum of O1s in Fig. 3 (d) can be divided into three peaks of 531.7, 532.5, and 533.3 eV corresponding to CO (24.58%), C–OH/C–O–C (44.96%), and –COOH (30.45%), respectively. It can be inferred from the XPS results that the P-CQDs comprised an aromatic polymer structure and were rich in oxygen-based groups such as the carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups; while, a pyrrole nitrogen was also present at the edge of the molecular.

The FT-IR spectrum was also detected, and the result was consistent with the XPS results. As shown in Fig. 3 (e), the wide peak at 3450 nm was the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH, indicating the existence of hydroxyl or carboxyl groups and amino groups in P-CQDs (Zhang et al., 2018). The weak peak at 2900 nm was related to the stretching vibration of C–H, which showed that a methoxide structure is present on the side chain of the benzene ring. The sharp peak at 1590 nm was the telescopic vibration peak of CC, indicating that there were numerous sp2 structures that enriched the graphene in the P-CQDs. These results are also consistent with those of TEM. In addition, the absorption peak at 1261 nm belonged to the stretching vibration of C–O–C, which might be related to the structure of lignin. The absorption peak between 1420 and 1480 nm was the bending vibration peak of OCH3.

Acknowledging the formation mechanism of CQDs can provide a theoretical basis for improving the yield of CQDs as well as explaining its photoluminescence properties. However, few studies have introduced how pine carbon quantum dots form an aromatic polymeric structure. Many studies have reported that (Wu et al., 2017) the natural lignocellulose compositions of pine, such as cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, will suffer from a condensation reaction, chain breaking, and chain forming reactions during the hydrothermal process. Thus, a special aromatic-polymer substance containing numerous oxygen groups and connected by a C–O and C–C bond was formed. Wu et al. tested the aromatic contents in pine wood liquid products through an NMR after hydrothermal treatment without a dialysis, the results of which suggest that the contents of aromatic C–H are decreased with an increase in the reaction temperature. However, in this study, a decrease in the aromatic C–H bond is attributed to the formation of aromatic polymeric structure. Because aromatic ring has a special six-membered ring structure and C–H can only be found on the carbon atoms at the edges of the ring, the C–H content was lower, and the aromatic polymeric structure on the P-CQDs was richer. We proposed a formation mechanism of the aromatic polymeric structure of P-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4 . Under high temperature and pressure, the cellulose macromolecular chains in pine wood are decomposed into glucose monomers, and further decomposed into furan acid, furan alcohol, polyphenol, and other structures. Hemicellulose was decomposed into furfural and furfuryl alcohol. In addition, the guaiacyl unit in lignin was degraded into various small molecules such as aromatic acids, phenols, and aromatic ethers. Meanwhile, condensation, chain breaking, and chain forming reactions occur in these hydrolytic products, and a series of heterocyclic compounds with polycyclic rings and a small amount of polyacid and polyiodides are formed. These heterocyclic compounds undergo a further polymerisation and condensation reaction. In addition, an aromatic condensation with numerous oxygen-containing functional groups is formed by connecting the C–C and C–O bonds, which could be considered as a precursor of the P-CQDs. Further increasing the reaction time, these condensates are contracted and connected to each other to form the spherical P-CQDs, which are characterized by its aromatic polymeric main body structure and being rich in oxygen-containing functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of P-CQDs during Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To determine which metal ions can be selectively identified by the P-CQDs, different metal ions (K+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+) were used to test their effects on the fluorescence intensity of P-CQDs at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The bar chart in Fig. 5 (a) shows a comparison of fluorescence intensities (F/F0, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity without the addition of metal ions) of P-CQDs after the addition of different metal ions. It can be found that, compared with other metal ions, only Fe3+ had a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of P-CQDs, whereas other metal ions only had a smaller effect. The reason for this might be the Fe3+ in water chelating with the hydroxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of P-CQDs, and the destruction of the radiation transition, causing a fluorescence quenching of the P-CQDs (Atchudan et al., 2018; Jia et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2017). These results indicate that the prepared P-CQDs have the ability to selectively detect Fe3+.

To determine the detection range of Fe3+ by the P-CQDs, we tested their fluorescence intensity by adding different concentrations of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 5 (b). When the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 0 to 2000 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of the P-CQDs gradually decreased, indicating that the fluorescence intensity was sensitive to the concentration of Fe3+. With an increase in the Fe3+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity decreased in a corresponding manner. When the concentration of Fe3+ was increased to 2000 μmol/L, the P-CQDs were distinctly quenched, and only 4.35% of the fluorescence intensity remained compared with the initial value. Fig. 5 (d) shows the changes in the visual fluorescence intensity after adding different concentrations of Fe3+ to the P-CQDs under irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. It can be seen that, as the amount of Fe3+ in the CQDs increased, their blue light gradually weakened. Fig. 4 (c) shows a good linear relationship between the Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence intensity, where the linear relation can be described by: F0/F = 0.00285 CFe3++0.9141(0–1000 μmol/L, R2 = 0.9820) and F0/F = 0.02257 CFe 3+ + 0.9141(1000–2000 μmol/L, R2 = 0.9872). The limit of detection was 355.4 nmol/L (LOD = 3σ/s).

In this study, fluorescent carbon quantum dots, which could be used for Fe3+ detection, were prepared from pine wood, a low-cost natural biomass resource, without adding any harmful agents and using only water as solvent. The whole preparation process was simple and green, and fully met the requirements of cleaner production. At the same time, as a traditional coniferous wood resources, the preparation of pine wood piece has formed a very complete industrial chain in the pulp and paper making industry, the large-scale production of P-CQDs has a good economic foundation. Low investment will yield high value P-CQDs, which can be seen as a high value by-product of a paper mill and the solid products after HTC process can still be used in the production of paper or activated carbon. The whole production process can provide some guidance for the high value utilisation of biomass materials.

P-CQDs can emit bright blue light at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm. However, P-CQDs exhibits poor fluorescence performance at other excitation wavelengths and the quantum yield is low, which limits some of its applications, such as cell imaging. Also, although P-CQDs can selectively detect Fe3+ ions, it is difficult to recycle them, which lead to frequent sample replacement in practical use, causing inconvenience. In the following research, the effects of the various components of pine wood on excitation wavelength in hydrothermal carbonisation reaction at different HTC conditions will be the focus of the study, so as to realize the self-regulation of the excitation wavelength and increase quantum yield, which will broaden the application field of P-CQDs. The fluorescence recovery of P-CQDs after quenching by Fe3+ will also be explored, which will make it recyclable.

In summary, P-CQDs were successfully prepared from pine wood, a low-value biomass resource, using a simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment without adding any toxic or harmful reagents. The P-CQDs also demonstrated a good water solubility, fluorescence, and optical stability. In addition, the formation mechanism of P-CQDs during the HTC process was deduced, and the P-CQDs had an aromatic polymeric structure rich in oxygenated functional groups. The P-CQDs can also selectively identify Fe3+ based on its fluorescence quenching, and thus can be used as a Fe3+ fluorescence sensor, which will have a broad application in Fe3+ detection. More important, compared with other reported methods, the production cost of CQDs is effectively reduced, enabling their easier application to the mass preparation of a Fe3+ fluorescent sensor.

Siyu Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xueping Song: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xinyu Chai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis. Peitao Zhao: Writing - review & editing. Hui He: Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Zhenwei Liu: Software, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We, the authors of manuscript of Green production of fluorescent carbon quantum dots based on pine wood and their application in the detection of Fe3+, claimed that this work is unpublished and not being considered elsewhere. All authors listed in this manuscript agreed that it should be submitted to Journal of Cleaner Production.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant NO. 21766002 and 51706240).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Anbinder et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A study of the structural changes in a chitosan matrix produced by the adsorption of copper and chromium ions
Carbohydr. Polym.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe(3+) ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Atchudan, R., Edison, T., Aseer, K.R., Perumal, S., Karthik, N., Lee, Y.R., 2018. ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Borahan et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of lead in milk samples using vortex assisted deep eutectic solvent based liquid phase microextraction-slotted quartz tube-flame atomic absorption spectrometry system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Borahan, T., Unutkan, T., Turan, N.B., Turak, F., Bakirdere, S., 2019.
